an5.134:1.1.pli: “Pañcahi, bhikkhave, aṅgehi samannāgato rājā khattiyo muddhāvasitto yassaṁ yassaṁ disāyaṁ viharati, sakasmiṁyeva vijite viharati.
an5.134:1.1.eng: “Mendicants, with five factors an anointed aristocratic king lives in his own realm, no matter what region he lives in.

an5.134:2.2.pli: Idha, bhikkhave, rājā khattiyo muddhāvasitto ubhato sujāto hoti mātito ca pitito ca, saṁsuddhagahaṇiko, yāva sattamā pitāmahayugā akkhitto anupakkuṭṭho jātivādena;
an5.134:2.2.eng: An anointed aristocratic king is well born on both his mother’s and father’s side, of pure descent, with irrefutable and impeccable genealogy back to the seventh paternal generation.

an5.134:2.3.eng: He is rich, affluent, and wealthy, with a full treasury and storehouses.

an5.134:2.4.pli: balavā kho pana hoti caturaṅginiyā senāya samannāgato assavāya ovādapaṭikarāya;
an5.134:2.4.eng: He is powerful, having an army of four divisions that is obedient and carries out instructions.

an5.134:2.5.pli: pariṇāyako kho panassa hoti paṇḍito viyatto medhāvī paṭibalo atītānāgatapaccuppanne atthe cintetuṁ;
an5.134:2.5.eng: He has a commander who is astute, competent, and intelligent, able to think issues through as they bear upon the past, future, and present.

an5.134:2.6.eng: These four things bring his glory to fruition.

an5.134:2.7.pli: So iminā yasapañcamena dhammena samannāgato yassaṁ yassaṁ disāyaṁ viharati, sakasmiṁyeva vijite viharati.
an5.134:2.7.eng: With these five qualities, including glory, an anointed aristocratic king lives in his own realm, no matter what direction he lives in.

an5.134:2.9.eng: Because that is how it is for victors.

an5.134:3.1.pli: Evamevaṁ kho, bhikkhave, pañcahi dhammehi samannāgato bhikkhu yassaṁ yassaṁ disāyaṁ viharati, vimuttacittova viharati.
an5.134:3.1.eng: In the same way, a mendicant with five qualities lives with mind freed, no matter what region they live in.

an5.134:3.3.pli: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu sīlavā hoti, pātimokkhasaṁvarasaṁvuto viharati ācāragocarasampanno aṇumattesu vajjesu bhayadassāvī, samādāya sikkhati sikkhāpadesu—
an5.134:3.3.eng: It’s when mendicant is ethical, restrained in the monastic code, conducting themselves well and resorting for alms in suitable places. Seeing danger in the slightest fault, they keep the rules they’ve undertaken.

an5.134:3.4.eng: This is like the anointed aristocratic king’s impeccable birth.

an5.134:3.5.pli: bahussuto hoti sutadharo sutasannicayo, ye te dhammā ādikalyāṇā majjhekalyāṇā pariyosānakalyāṇā sātthaṁ sabyañjanaṁ kevalaparipuṇṇaṁ parisuddhaṁ brahmacariyaṁ abhivadanti, tathārūpāssa dhammā bahussutā honti dhātā vacasā paricitā manasānupekkhitā diṭṭhiyā suppaṭividdhā—
an5.134:3.5.eng: They’re very learned, remembering and keeping what they’ve learned. These teachings are good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased, describing a spiritual practice that’s entirely full and pure. They are very learned in such teachings, remembering them, rehearsing them, mentally scrutinizing them, and penetrating them theoretically.

an5.134:3.6.pli: rājāva khattiyo muddhāvasitto aḍḍho mahaddhano mahābhogo paripuṇṇakosakoṭṭhāgāro;
an5.134:3.6.eng: This is like the anointed aristocratic king being rich, affluent, and wealthy, with full treasury and storehouses.

an5.134:3.7.pli: āraddhavīriyo viharati akusalānaṁ dhammānaṁ pahānāya kusalānaṁ dhammānaṁ upasampadāya thāmavā daḷhaparakkamo anikkhittadhuro kusalesu dhammesu—
an5.134:3.7.eng: They live with energy roused up for giving up unskillful qualities and embracing skillful qualities. They’re strong, staunchly vigorous, not slacking off when it comes to developing skillful qualities.

an5.134:3.8.eng: This is like the anointed aristocratic king having power.

an5.134:3.9.pli: paññavā hoti udayatthagāminiyā paññāya samannāgato ariyāya nibbedhikāya sammā dukkhakkhayagāminiyā—
an5.134:3.9.eng: They’re wise. They have the wisdom of arising and passing away which is noble, penetrative, and leads to the complete ending of suffering.

an5.134:3.10.eng: This is like the anointed aristocratic king having a commander.

an5.134:3.11.eng: These four qualities bring their freedom to fruition.

an5.134:3.12.pli: So iminā vimuttipañcamena dhammena samannāgato yassaṁ yassaṁ disāyaṁ viharati vimuttacittova viharati.
an5.134:3.12.eng: With these five qualities, including freedom, they live in their own realm, no matter what region they live in.

an5.134:3.14.eng: Because that is how it is for those whose mind is free.”

an5.135:1.1.pli: “Pañcahi, bhikkhave, aṅgehi samannāgato rañño khattiyassa muddhāvasittassa jeṭṭho putto rajjaṁ pattheti.
an5.135:1.1.eng: “Mendicants, an anointed aristocratic king’s eldest son with five factors aspires to kingship.

an5.135:1.3.pli: Idha, bhikkhave, rañño khattiyassa muddhāvasittassa jeṭṭho putto ubhato sujāto hoti mātito ca pitito ca, saṁsuddhagahaṇiko, yāva sattamā pitāmahayugā akkhitto anupakkuṭṭho jātivādena;
an5.135:1.3.eng: It’s when an anointed aristocratic king’s eldest son is well born on both his mother’s and father’s side, of pure descent, with irrefutable and impeccable genealogy back to the seventh paternal generation.

an5.135:1.4.pli: abhirūpo hoti dassanīyo pāsādiko paramāya vaṇṇapokkharatāya samannāgato;
an5.135:1.4.eng: He is attractive, good-looking, lovely, of surpassing beauty.

an5.135:1.5.eng: He is dear and beloved to his parents.

an5.135:1.6.eng: He is dear and beloved to the people of town and country.

an5.135:1.7.pli: yāni tāni raññaṁ khattiyānaṁ muddhāvasittānaṁ sippaṭṭhānāni hatthismiṁ vā assasmiṁ vā rathasmiṁ vā dhanusmiṁ vā tharusmiṁ vā tattha sikkhito hoti anavayo.
an5.135:1.7.eng: He is trained and skilled in the arts of anointed aristocratic kings, such as elephant riding, horse riding, driving a chariot, archery, and swordsmanship.

an5.135:2.2.pli: ‘ahaṁ khomhi ubhato sujāto mātito ca pitito ca, saṁsuddhagahaṇiko, yāva sattamā pitāmahayugā akkhitto anupakkuṭṭho jātivādena.
an5.135:2.2.eng: ‘I’m well born on both my mother’s and father’s side, of pure descent, with irrefutable and impeccable genealogy back to the seventh paternal generation.

an5.135:2.3.eng: Why shouldn’t I aspire to kingship?

an5.135:2.4.pli: Ahaṁ khomhi abhirūpo dassanīyo pāsādiko paramāya vaṇṇapokkharatāya samannāgato.
an5.135:2.4.eng: I’m attractive, good-looking, lovely, of surpassing beauty.

an5.135:2.5.eng: Why shouldn’t I aspire to kingship?

an5.135:2.6.eng: I’m dear and beloved to my parents.

an5.135:2.7.eng: Why shouldn’t I aspire to kingship?

an5.135:2.8.eng: I’m dear and beloved to the people of town and country.

an5.135:2.9.eng: Why shouldn’t I aspire to kingship?

an5.135:2.10.pli: Ahaṁ khomhi yāni tāni raññaṁ khattiyānaṁ muddhāvasittānaṁ sippaṭṭhānāni hatthismiṁ vā assasmiṁ vā rathasmiṁ vā dhanusmiṁ vā tharusmiṁ vā, tattha sikkhito anavayo.
an5.135:2.10.eng: I’m trained and skilled in the arts of anointed aristocratic kings, such as elephant riding, horse riding, driving a chariot, archery, and swordsmanship.

an5.135:2.11.eng: Why shouldn’t I aspire to kingship?’

an5.135:2.12.pli: Imehi kho, bhikkhave, pañcahi aṅgehi samannāgato rañño khattiyassa muddhāvasittassa jeṭṭho putto rajjaṁ pattheti.
an5.135:2.12.eng: An anointed aristocratic king’s eldest son with these five factors aspires to kingship.

an5.135:3.1.pli: Evamevaṁ kho, bhikkhave, pañcahi dhammehi samannāgato bhikkhu āsavānaṁ khayaṁ pattheti.
an5.135:3.1.eng: In the same way, a mendicant with five qualities aspires to end the defilements.

an5.135:3.3.pli: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu saddho hoti, saddahati tathāgatassa bodhiṁ:
an5.135:3.3.eng: It’s when a mendicant has faith in the Realized One’s awakening:

an5.135:3.4.pli: ‘itipi so bhagavā arahaṁ sammāsambuddho vijjācaraṇasampanno sugato lokavidū anuttaro purisadammasārathi satthā devamanussānaṁ buddho bhagavā’ti.
an5.135:3.4.eng: ‘That Blessed One is perfected, a fully awakened Buddha, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, holy, knower of the world, supreme guide for those fit for training, teacher of gods and humans, awakened, blessed.’

an5.135:3.5.pli: Appābādho hoti appātaṅko, samavepākiniyā gahaṇiyā samannāgato nātisītāya nāccuṇhāya majjhimāya padhānakkhamāya;
an5.135:3.5.eng: They are rarely ill or unwell. Their stomach digests well, being neither too hot nor too cold, but just right, and fit for meditation.

an5.135:3.6.pli: asaṭho hoti amāyāvī, yathābhūtaṁ attānaṁ āvikattā satthari vā viññūsu vā sabrahmacārīsu;
an5.135:3.6.eng: They’re not devious or deceitful. They reveal themselves honestly to the Teacher or sensible spiritual companions.

an5.135:3.7.pli: āraddhavīriyo viharati akusalānaṁ dhammānaṁ pahānāya, kusalānaṁ dhammānaṁ upasampadāya, thāmavā daḷhaparakkamo anikkhittadhuro kusalesu dhammesu;
an5.135:3.7.eng: They live with energy roused up for giving up unskillful qualities and embracing skillful qualities. They’re strong, staunchly vigorous, not slacking off when it comes to developing skillful qualities.

an5.135:3.8.pli: paññavā hoti udayatthagāminiyā paññāya samannāgato ariyāya nibbedhikāya sammā dukkhakkhayagāminiyā.
an5.135:3.8.eng: They’re wise. They have the wisdom of arising and passing away which is noble, penetrative, and leads to the complete ending of suffering.

an5.135:4.2.eng: ‘I am a person of faith; I have faith in the Realized One’s awakening …

an5.135:4.3.pli: “itipi so bhagavā arahaṁ sammāsambuddho …pe… satthā devamanussānaṁ buddho bhagavā”ti.

an5.135:4.4.eng: Why shouldn’t I aspire to end the defilements?

an5.135:4.5.pli: Ahaṁ khomhi appābādho appātaṅko samavepākiniyā gahaṇiyā samannāgato nātisītāya nāccuṇhāya majjhimāya padhānakkhamāya.
an5.135:4.5.eng: I’m rarely ill or unwell. My stomach digests well, being neither too hot nor too cold, but just right, and fit for meditation.

an5.135:4.6.eng: Why shouldn’t I aspire to end the defilements?

an5.135:4.7.pli: Ahaṁ khomhi asaṭho amāyāvī yathābhūtaṁ attānaṁ āvikattā satthari vā viññūsu vā sabrahmacārīsu.
an5.135:4.7.eng: I’m not devious or deceitful. I reveal myself honestly to the Teacher or sensible spiritual companions.

an5.135:4.8.eng: Why shouldn’t I aspire to end the defilements?

an5.135:4.9.pli: Ahaṁ khomhi āraddhavīriyo viharāmi akusalānaṁ dhammānaṁ pahānāya, kusalānaṁ dhammānaṁ upasampadāya, thāmavā daḷhaparakkamo anikkhittadhuro kusalesu dhammesu.
an5.135:4.9.eng: I live with energy roused up for giving up unskillful qualities and embracing skillful qualities. I’m strong, staunchly vigorous, not slacking off when it comes to developing skillful qualities.

an5.135:4.10.eng: Why shouldn’t I aspire to end the defilements?

an5.135:4.11.pli: Ahaṁ khomhi paññavā udayatthagāminiyā paññāya samannāgato ariyāya nibbedhikāya sammā dukkhakkhayagāminiyā.
an5.135:4.11.eng: I’m wise. I have the wisdom of arising and passing away which is noble, penetrative, and leads to the complete ending of suffering.

an5.135:4.12.eng: Why shouldn’t I aspire to end the defilements?’

an5.135:4.13.pli: Imehi kho, bhikkhave, pañcahi dhammehi samannāgato bhikkhu āsavānaṁ khayaṁ patthetī”ti.
an5.135:4.13.eng: A mendicant with these five qualities aspires to end the defilements.”

an5.136:1.1.pli: “Pañcahi, bhikkhave, aṅgehi samannāgato rañño khattiyassa muddhāvasittassa jeṭṭho putto oparajjaṁ pattheti.
an5.136:1.1.eng: “Mendicants, an anointed aristocratic king’s eldest son with five factors aspires to become a viceroy.

an5.136:1.3.pli: Idha, bhikkhave, rañño khattiyassa muddhāvasittassa jeṭṭho putto ubhato sujāto hoti mātito ca pitito ca, saṁsuddhagahaṇiko, yāva sattamā pitāmahayugā akkhitto anupakkuṭṭho jātivādena;
an5.136:1.3.eng: It’s when an anointed aristocratic king’s eldest son is well born on both his mother’s and father’s side, of pure descent, with irrefutable and impeccable genealogy back to the seventh paternal generation.

an5.136:1.4.pli: abhirūpo hoti dassanīyo pāsādiko paramāya vaṇṇapokkharatāya samannāgato;
an5.136:1.4.eng: He is attractive, good-looking, lovely, of surpassing beauty.

an5.136:1.5.eng: He is dear and beloved to his parents.

an5.136:1.6.eng: He is dear and beloved to the armed forces.

an5.136:1.7.pli: paṇḍito hoti viyatto medhāvī paṭibalo atītānāgatapaccuppanne atthe cintetuṁ.
an5.136:1.7.eng: He is astute, competent, and intelligent, able to think issues through as they bear upon the past, future, and present.

an5.136:2.2.pli: ‘ahaṁ khomhi ubhato sujāto mātito ca pitito ca, saṁsuddhagahaṇiko, yāva sattamā pitāmahayugā akkhitto anupakkuṭṭho jātivādena.

an5.136:2.4.pli: Ahaṁ khomhi abhirūpo dassanīyo pāsādiko paramāya vaṇṇapokkharatāya samannāgato.

an5.136:2.6.eng: dear and beloved to my parents …

an5.136:2.8.eng: dear and beloved to the armed forces …

an5.136:2.10.pli: Ahaṁ khomhi paṇḍito viyatto medhāvī paṭibalo atītānāgatapaccuppanne atthe cintetuṁ.
an5.136:2.10.eng: I’m astute, competent, and intelligent, able to think issues through as they bear upon the past, future, and present.

an5.136:2.11.eng: Why shouldn’t I aspire to become a viceroy?’

an5.136:2.12.pli: Imehi kho, bhikkhave, pañcahi aṅgehi samannāgato rañño khattiyassa muddhāvasittassa jeṭṭho putto oparajjaṁ pattheti.
an5.136:2.12.eng: An anointed aristocratic king’s eldest son with these five factors aspires to become a viceroy.

an5.136:3.1.pli: Evamevaṁ kho, bhikkhave, pañcahi dhammehi samannāgato bhikkhu āsavānaṁ khayaṁ pattheti.
an5.136:3.1.eng: In the same way, a mendicant with five qualities aspires to end the defilements.

an5.136:3.3.pli: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu sīlavā hoti …pe… samādāya sikkhati sikkhāpadesu;
an5.136:3.3.eng: It’s when a mendicant is ethical, restrained in the monastic code, conducting themselves well and resorting for alms in suitable places. Seeing danger in the slightest fault, they keep the rules they’ve undertaken.

an5.136:3.4.eng: They’re very learned, remembering and keeping what they’ve learned. These teachings are good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased, describing a spiritual practice that’s totally full and pure. They are very learned in such teachings, remembering them, reciting them, mentally scrutinizing them, and penetrating them theoretically.

an5.136:3.5.eng: Their mind is firmly established in the four kinds of mindfulness meditation.

an5.136:3.6.pli: āraddhavīriyo viharati akusalānaṁ dhammānaṁ pahānāya, kusalānaṁ dhammānaṁ upasampadāya, thāmavā daḷhaparakkamo anikkhittadhuro kusalesu dhammesu;
an5.136:3.6.eng: They live with energy roused up for giving up unskillful qualities and embracing skillful qualities. They’re strong, staunchly vigorous, not slacking off when it comes to developing skillful qualities.

an5.136:3.7.pli: paññavā hoti, udayatthagāminiyā paññāya samannāgato ariyāya nibbedhikāya sammā dukkhakkhayagāminiyā.
an5.136:3.7.eng: They’re wise. They have the wisdom of arising and passing away which is noble, penetrative, and leads to the complete ending of suffering.

an5.136:4.2.pli: ‘ahaṁ khomhi sīlavā, pātimokkhasaṁvarasaṁvuto viharāmi ācāragocarasampanno aṇumattesu vajjesu bhayadassāvī, samādāya sikkhāmi sikkhāpadesu.

an5.136:4.4.pli: Ahaṁ khomhi bahussuto sutadharo sutasannicayo, ye te dhammā ādikalyāṇā majjhekalyāṇā pariyosānakalyāṇā sātthaṁ sabyañjanaṁ kevalaparipuṇṇaṁ parisuddhaṁ brahmacariyaṁ abhivadanti, tathārūpā me dhammā bahussutā honti dhātā vacasā paricitā manasānupekkhitā diṭṭhiyā suppaṭividdhā.

an5.136:4.8.pli: Ahaṁ khomhi āraddhavīriyo viharāmi akusalānaṁ dhammānaṁ pahānāya, kusalānaṁ dhammānaṁ upasampadāya, thāmavā daḷhaparakkamo anikkhittadhuro kusalesu dhammesu.

an5.136:4.10.pli: Ahaṁ khomhi paññavā udayatthagāminiyā paññāya samannāgato ariyāya nibbedhikāya sammā dukkhakkhayagāminiyā.
an5.136:4.10.eng: wise. I have the wisdom of arising and passing away which is noble, penetrative, and leads to the complete ending of suffering.

an5.136:4.11.eng: Why shouldn’t I aspire to end the defilements?’

an5.136:4.12.pli: Imehi kho, bhikkhave, pañcahi dhammehi samannāgato bhikkhu āsavānaṁ khayaṁ patthetī”ti.
an5.136:4.12.eng: A mendicant with these five qualities aspires to end the defilements.”

an5.137:1.1.pli: “Pañcime, bhikkhave, appaṁ rattiyā supanti, bahuṁ jagganti.
an5.137:1.1.eng: “Mendicants, these five sleep little at night, staying mostly awake.

an5.137:1.3.pli: Itthī, bhikkhave, purisādhippāyā appaṁ rattiyā supati, bahuṁ jaggati.
an5.137:1.3.eng: A woman longing for a man.

an5.137:1.4.pli: Puriso, bhikkhave, itthādhippāyo appaṁ rattiyā supati, bahuṁ jaggati.
an5.137:1.4.eng: A man longing for a woman.

an5.137:1.5.pli: Coro, bhikkhave, ādānādhippāyo appaṁ rattiyā supati, bahuṁ jaggati.
an5.137:1.5.eng: A thief longing for their loot.

an5.137:1.6.pli: Rājā, bhikkhave, rājakaraṇīyesu yutto appaṁ rattiyā supati, bahuṁ jaggati.
an5.137:1.6.eng: A king busy with his duties.

an5.137:1.7.pli: Bhikkhu, bhikkhave, visaṁyogādhippāyo appaṁ rattiyā supati, bahuṁ jaggati.
an5.137:1.7.eng: A mendicant longing for freedom from the yoke.

an5.137:1.8.pli: Ime kho, bhikkhave, pañca appaṁ rattiyā supanti, bahuṁ jaggantī”ti.
an5.137:1.8.eng: These five sleep little at night, staying mostly awake.”

an5.138:1.1.pli: “Pañcahi, bhikkhave, aṅgehi samannāgato rañño nāgo bhattādako ca hoti okāsapharaṇo ca laṇḍasāraṇo ca salākaggāhī ca rañño nāgotveva saṅkhaṁ gacchati.
an5.138:1.1.eng: “Mendicants, a royal bull elephant with five factors eats food, takes up space, drops dung, and takes a ticket, yet is still reckoned a royal bull elephant.

an5.138:1.3.pli: Idha, bhikkhave, rañño nāgo akkhamo hoti rūpānaṁ, akkhamo saddānaṁ, akkhamo gandhānaṁ, akkhamo rasānaṁ, akkhamo phoṭṭhabbānaṁ.
an5.138:1.3.eng: It’s when a royal bull elephant can’t endure sights, sounds, smells, tastes, and touches.

an5.138:1.4.pli: Imehi kho, bhikkhave, pañcahi aṅgehi samannāgato rañño nāgo bhattādako ca okāsapharaṇo ca laṇḍasāraṇo ca salākaggāhī ca, rañño nāgotveva saṅkhaṁ gacchati.
an5.138:1.4.eng: A royal bull elephant with these five factors eats food, takes up space, drops dung, and takes a ticket, yet is still reckoned a royal bull elephant.

an5.138:2.1.pli: Evamevaṁ kho, bhikkhave, pañcahi dhammehi samannāgato bhikkhu bhattādako ca hoti, okāsapharaṇo ca mañcapīṭhamaddano ca salākaggāhī ca, bhikkhutveva saṅkhaṁ gacchati.
an5.138:2.1.eng: In the same way, a mendicant with five qualities eats food, takes up space, tramples beds and chairs, and takes a ticket, yet is still reckoned a mendicant.

an5.138:2.3.pli: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu akkhamo hoti rūpānaṁ, akkhamo saddānaṁ, akkhamo gandhānaṁ, akkhamo rasānaṁ, akkhamo phoṭṭhabbānaṁ.
an5.138:2.3.eng: It’s when a mendicant can’t endure sights, sounds, smells, tastes, and touches.

an5.138:2.4.pli: Imehi kho, bhikkhave, pañcahi dhammehi samannāgato bhikkhu bhattādako ca hoti okāsapharaṇo ca mañcapīṭhamaddano ca salākaggāhī ca, bhikkhutveva saṅkhaṁ gacchatī”ti.
an5.138:2.4.eng: A mendicant with these five qualities eats food, takes up space, tramples beds and chairs, and takes a ticket, yet is still reckoned a mendicant.”

an5.139:1.1.pli: “Pañcahi, bhikkhave, aṅgehi samannāgato rañño nāgo na rājāraho hoti na rājabhoggo, na rañño aṅgaṁtveva saṅkhaṁ gacchati.
an5.139:1.1.eng: “Mendicants, a royal bull elephant with five factors is not worthy of a king, not fit to serve a king, and is not reckoned a factor of kingship.

an5.139:1.3.pli: Idha, bhikkhave, rañño nāgo akkhamo hoti rūpānaṁ, akkhamo saddānaṁ, akkhamo gandhānaṁ, akkhamo rasānaṁ, akkhamo phoṭṭhabbānaṁ.
an5.139:1.3.eng: It’s when a royal bull elephant can’t endure sights, sounds, smells, tastes, and touches.

an5.139:2.1.pli: Kathañca, bhikkhave, rañño nāgo akkhamo hoti rūpānaṁ?
an5.139:2.1.eng: And how is it that a royal bull elephant can’t endure sights?

an5.139:2.2.pli: Idha, bhikkhave, rañño nāgo saṅgāmagato hatthikāyaṁ vā disvā assakāyaṁ vā disvā rathakāyaṁ vā disvā pattikāyaṁ vā disvā saṁsīdati visīdati, na santhambhati na sakkoti saṅgāmaṁ otarituṁ.
an5.139:2.2.eng: It’s when a royal bull elephant gone to battle falters and founders at the sight of a division of elephants, of cavalry, of chariots, or of infantry. It doesn’t stay firm, and fails to plunge into battle.

an5.139:2.3.pli: Evaṁ kho, bhikkhave, rañño nāgo akkhamo hoti rūpānaṁ.
an5.139:2.3.eng: That’s how a royal bull elephant can’t endure sights.

an5.139:3.1.pli: Kathañca, bhikkhave, rañño nāgo akkhamo hoti saddānaṁ?
an5.139:3.1.eng: And how is it that a royal bull elephant can’t endure sounds?

an5.139:3.2.pli: Idha, bhikkhave, rañño nāgo saṅgāmagato hatthisaddaṁ vā sutvā assasaddaṁ vā sutvā rathasaddaṁ vā sutvā pattisaddaṁ vā sutvā bheripaṇavasaṅkhatiṇavaninnādasaddaṁ vā sutvā saṁsīdati visīdati, na santhambhati na sakkoti saṅgāmaṁ otarituṁ.
an5.139:3.2.eng: It’s when a royal bull elephant gone to battle falters and founders at the sound of a division of elephants, of cavalry, of chariots, or of infantry, or the thunder of the drums, kettledrums, horns, and cymbals. It doesn’t stay firm, and fails to plunge into battle.

an5.139:3.3.pli: Evaṁ kho, bhikkhave, rañño nāgo akkhamo hoti saddānaṁ.
an5.139:3.3.eng: That’s how a royal bull elephant can’t endure sounds.

an5.139:4.1.pli: Kathañca, bhikkhave, rañño nāgo akkhamo hoti gandhānaṁ?
an5.139:4.1.eng: And how is it that a royal bull elephant can’t endure smells?

an5.139:4.2.pli: Idha, bhikkhave, rañño nāgo saṅgāmagato ye te rañño nāgā abhijātā saṅgāmāvacarā tesaṁ muttakarīsassa gandhaṁ ghāyitvā saṁsīdati visīdati, na santhambhati na sakkoti saṅgāmaṁ otarituṁ.
an5.139:4.2.eng: It’s when a royal bull elephant gone to battle falters and founders when it smells the odor of the feces and urine of battle-hardened, pedigree royal bull elephants. It doesn’t stay firm, and fails to plunge into battle.

an5.139:4.3.pli: Evaṁ kho, bhikkhave, rañño nāgo akkhamo hoti gandhānaṁ.
an5.139:4.3.eng: That’s how a royal bull elephant can’t endure smells.

an5.139:5.1.pli: Kathañca, bhikkhave, rañño nāgo akkhamo hoti rasānaṁ?
an5.139:5.1.eng: And how is it that a royal bull elephant can’t endure tastes?

an5.139:5.2.pli: Idha, bhikkhave, rañño nāgo saṅgāmagato ekissā vā tiṇodakadattiyā vimānito dvīhi vā tīhi vā catūhi vā pañcahi vā tiṇodakadattīhi vimānito saṁsīdati visīdati, na santhambhati na sakkoti saṅgāmaṁ otarituṁ.
an5.139:5.2.eng: It’s when a royal bull elephant gone to battle falters and founders when it misses a meal of grass and water, or it misses two, three, four, or five meals. It doesn’t stay firm, and fails to plunge into battle.

an5.139:5.3.pli: Evaṁ kho, bhikkhave, rañño nāgo akkhamo hoti rasānaṁ.
an5.139:5.3.eng: That’s how a royal bull elephant can’t endure tastes.

an5.139:6.1.pli: Kathañca, bhikkhave, rañño nāgo akkhamo hoti phoṭṭhabbānaṁ?
an5.139:6.1.eng: And how is it that a royal bull elephant can’t endure touches?

an5.139:6.2.pli: Idha, bhikkhave, rañño nāgo saṅgāmagato ekena vā saravegena viddho, dvīhi vā tīhi vā catūhi vā pañcahi vā saravegehi viddho saṁsīdati visīdati, na santhambhati na sakkoti saṅgāmaṁ otarituṁ.
an5.139:6.2.eng: It’s when a royal bull elephant gone to battle falters and founders when struck by a swift arrow, or by two, three, four, or five swift arrows. It doesn’t stay firm, and fails to plunge into battle.

an5.139:6.3.pli: Evaṁ kho, bhikkhave, rañño nāgo akkhamo hoti phoṭṭhabbānaṁ.
an5.139:6.3.eng: That’s how a royal bull elephant can’t endure touches.

an5.139:7.1.pli: Imehi kho, bhikkhave, pañcahi aṅgehi samannāgato rañño nāgo na rājāraho hoti na rājabhoggo na rañño aṅgaṁtveva saṅkhaṁ gacchati.
an5.139:7.1.eng: A royal bull elephant with these five factors is not worthy of a king, not fit to serve a king, and is not reckoned a factor of kingship.

an5.139:8.1.pli: Evamevaṁ kho, bhikkhave, pañcahi aṅgehi samannāgato bhikkhu na āhuneyyo hoti na pāhuneyyo na dakkhiṇeyyo na añjalikaraṇīyo na anuttaraṁ puññakkhettaṁ lokassa.
an5.139:8.1.eng: In the same way, a mendicant with five qualities is not worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, not worthy of hospitality, not worthy of a religious donation, not worthy of veneration with joined palms, and is not the supreme field of merit for the world.

an5.139:8.3.pli: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu akkhamo hoti rūpānaṁ, akkhamo saddānaṁ, akkhamo gandhānaṁ, akkhamo rasānaṁ, akkhamo phoṭṭhabbānaṁ.
an5.139:8.3.eng: It’s when a mendicant can’t endure sights, sounds, smells, tastes, and touches.

an5.139:9.1.pli: Kathañca, bhikkhave, bhikkhu akkhamo hoti rūpānaṁ?
an5.139:9.1.eng: And how is it that a mendicant can’t endure sights?

an5.139:9.2.pli: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu cakkhunā rūpaṁ disvā rajanīye rūpe sārajjati, na sakkoti cittaṁ samādahituṁ.
an5.139:9.2.eng: It’s when a mendicant, seeing a sight with their eyes, is aroused by a desirable sight, so is not able to still the mind.

an5.139:9.3.pli: Evaṁ kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhu akkhamo hoti rūpānaṁ.
an5.139:9.3.eng: That’s how a mendicant can’t endure sights.

an5.139:10.1.pli: Kathañca, bhikkhave, bhikkhu akkhamo hoti saddānaṁ?
an5.139:10.1.eng: And how is it that a mendicant can’t endure sounds?

an5.139:10.2.pli: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu sotena saddaṁ sutvā rajanīye sadde sārajjati, na sakkoti cittaṁ samādahituṁ.
an5.139:10.2.eng: It’s when a mendicant, hearing a sound with their ears, is aroused by a desirable sound, so is not able to still the mind.

an5.139:10.3.pli: Evaṁ kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhu akkhamo hoti saddānaṁ.
an5.139:10.3.eng: That’s how a mendicant can’t endure sounds.

an5.139:11.1.pli: Kathañca, bhikkhave, bhikkhu akkhamo hoti gandhānaṁ?
an5.139:11.1.eng: And how is it that a mendicant can’t endure smells?

an5.139:11.2.pli: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu ghānena gandhaṁ ghāyitvā rajanīye gandhe sārajjati, na sakkoti cittaṁ samādahituṁ.
an5.139:11.2.eng: It’s when a mendicant, smelling an odor with their nose, is aroused by a desirable smell, so is not able to still the mind.

an5.139:11.3.pli: Evaṁ kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhu akkhamo hoti gandhānaṁ.
an5.139:11.3.eng: That’s how a mendicant can’t endure smells.

an5.139:12.1.pli: Kathañca, bhikkhave, bhikkhu akkhamo hoti rasānaṁ?
an5.139:12.1.eng: And how is it that a mendicant can’t endure tastes?

an5.139:12.2.pli: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu jivhāya rasaṁ sāyitvā rajanīye rase sārajjati, na sakkoti cittaṁ samādahituṁ.
an5.139:12.2.eng: It’s when a mendicant, tasting a flavor with their tongue, is aroused by desirable tastes, so is not able to still the mind.

an5.139:12.3.pli: Evaṁ kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhu akkhamo hoti rasānaṁ.
an5.139:12.3.eng: That’s how a mendicant can’t endure tastes.

an5.139:13.1.pli: Kathañca, bhikkhave, bhikkhu akkhamo hoti phoṭṭhabbānaṁ?
an5.139:13.1.eng: And how is it that a mendicant can’t endure touches?

an5.139:13.2.pli: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu kāyena phoṭṭhabbaṁ phusitvā rajanīye phoṭṭhabbe sārajjati, na sakkoti cittaṁ samādahituṁ.
an5.139:13.2.eng: It’s when a mendicant, feeling a touch with their body, is aroused by a desirable touch, so is not able to still the mind.

an5.139:13.3.pli: Evaṁ kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhu akkhamo hoti phoṭṭhabbānaṁ.
an5.139:13.3.eng: That’s how a mendicant can’t endure touches.

an5.139:14.1.pli: Imehi kho, bhikkhave, pañcahi dhammehi samannāgato bhikkhu na āhuneyyo hoti na pāhuneyyo na dakkhiṇeyyo na añjalikaraṇīyo na anuttaraṁ puññakkhettaṁ lokassa.
an5.139:14.1.eng: A mendicant with these five qualities is not worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, not worthy of hospitality, not worthy of a religious donation, not worthy of veneration with joined palms, and is not the supreme field of merit for the world.

an5.139:15.1.pli: Pañcahi, bhikkhave, aṅgehi samannāgato rañño nāgo rājāraho hoti rājabhoggo, rañño aṅgaṁtveva saṅkhaṁ gacchati.
an5.139:15.1.eng: A royal bull elephant with five factors is worthy of a king, fit to serve a king, and is reckoned a factor of kingship.

an5.139:15.3.pli: Idha, bhikkhave, rañño nāgo khamo hoti rūpānaṁ, khamo saddānaṁ, khamo gandhānaṁ, khamo rasānaṁ, khamo phoṭṭhabbānaṁ.
an5.139:15.3.eng: It’s when a royal bull elephant can endure sights, sounds, smells, tastes, and touches.

an5.139:16.1.pli: Kathañca, bhikkhave, rañño nāgo khamo hoti rūpānaṁ?
an5.139:16.1.eng: And how is it that a royal bull elephant can endure sights?

an5.139:16.2.pli: Idha, bhikkhave, rañño nāgo saṅgāmagato hatthikāyaṁ vā disvā assakāyaṁ vā disvā rathakāyaṁ vā disvā pattikāyaṁ vā disvā na saṁsīdati na visīdati, santhambhati sakkoti saṅgāmaṁ otarituṁ.
an5.139:16.2.eng: It’s when a royal bull elephant gone to battle does not falter or founder at the sight of a division of elephants, of cavalry, of chariots, or of infantry. It stays firm, and plunges into battle.

an5.139:16.3.pli: Evaṁ kho, bhikkhave, rañño nāgo khamo hoti rūpānaṁ.
an5.139:16.3.eng: That’s how a royal bull elephant can endure sights.

an5.139:17.1.pli: Kathañca, bhikkhave, rañño nāgo khamo hoti saddānaṁ?
an5.139:17.1.eng: And how is it that a royal bull elephant can endure sounds?

an5.139:17.2.pli: Idha, bhikkhave, rañño nāgo saṅgāmagato hatthisaddaṁ vā sutvā assasaddaṁ vā sutvā rathasaddaṁ vā sutvā pattisaddaṁ vā sutvā bheripaṇavasaṅkhatiṇavaninnādasaddaṁ vā sutvā na saṁsīdati na visīdati, santhambhati sakkoti saṅgāmaṁ otarituṁ.
an5.139:17.2.eng: It’s when a royal bull elephant does not falter or founder at the sound of a division of elephants, of cavalry, of chariots, or of infantry, or the thunder of the drums, kettledrums, horns, and cymbals. It stays firm, and plunges into battle.

an5.139:17.3.pli: Evaṁ kho, bhikkhave, rañño nāgo khamo hoti saddānaṁ.
an5.139:17.3.eng: That’s how a royal bull elephant can endure sounds.

an5.139:18.1.pli: Kathañca, bhikkhave, rañño nāgo khamo hoti gandhānaṁ?
an5.139:18.1.eng: And how is it that a royal bull elephant can endure smells?

an5.139:18.2.pli: Idha, bhikkhave, rañño nāgo saṅgāmagato ye te rañño nāgā abhijātā saṅgāmāvacarā tesaṁ muttakarīsassa gandhaṁ ghāyitvā na saṁsīdati na visīdati, santhambhati sakkoti saṅgāmaṁ otarituṁ.
an5.139:18.2.eng: It’s when a royal bull elephant gone to battle does not falter or founder when it smells the odor of the feces and urine of battle-hardened, pedigree royal bull elephants. It stays firm, and plunges into battle.

an5.139:18.3.pli: Evaṁ kho, bhikkhave, rañño nāgo khamo hoti gandhānaṁ.
an5.139:18.3.eng: That’s how a royal bull elephant can endure smells.

an5.139:19.1.pli: Kathañca, bhikkhave, rañño nāgo khamo hoti rasānaṁ?
an5.139:19.1.eng: And how is it that a royal bull elephant can endure tastes?

an5.139:19.2.pli: Idha, bhikkhave, rañño nāgo saṅgāmagato ekissā vā tiṇodakadattiyā vimānito dvīhi vā tīhi vā catūhi vā pañcahi vā tiṇodakadattīhi vimānito na saṁsīdati na visīdati, santhambhati sakkoti saṅgāmaṁ otarituṁ.
an5.139:19.2.eng: It’s when a royal bull elephant gone to battle does not falter or founder when it misses a meal of grass and water, or it misses two, three, four, or five meals. It stays firm, and plunges into battle.

an5.139:19.3.pli: Evaṁ kho, bhikkhave, rañño nāgo khamo hoti rasānaṁ.
an5.139:19.3.eng: That’s how a royal bull elephant can endure tastes.

an5.139:20.1.pli: Kathañca, bhikkhave, rañño nāgo khamo hoti phoṭṭhabbānaṁ?
an5.139:20.1.eng: And how is it that a royal bull elephant can endure touches?

an5.139:20.2.pli: Idha, bhikkhave, rañño nāgo saṅgāmagato ekena vā saravegena viddho, dvīhi vā tīhi vā catūhi vā pañcahi vā saravegehi viddho na saṁsīdati na visīdati, santhambhati sakkoti saṅgāmaṁ otarituṁ.
an5.139:20.2.eng: It’s when a royal bull elephant gone to battle does not falter or founder when struck by a swift arrow, or by two, three, four, or five swift arrows. It stays firm, and plunges into battle.

an5.139:20.3.pli: Evaṁ kho, bhikkhave, rañño nāgo khamo hoti phoṭṭhabbānaṁ.
an5.139:20.3.eng: That’s how a royal bull elephant can endure touches.

an5.139:21.1.pli: Imehi kho, bhikkhave, pañcahi aṅgehi samannāgato rañño nāgo rājāraho hoti rājabhoggo, rañño aṅgaṁtveva saṅkhaṁ gacchati.
an5.139:21.1.eng: A royal bull elephant with these five factors is worthy of a king, fit to serve a king, and is reckoned a factor of kingship.

an5.139:22.1.pli: Evamevaṁ kho, bhikkhave, pañcahi dhammehi samannāgato bhikkhu āhuneyyo hoti pāhuneyyo dakkhiṇeyyo añjalikaraṇīyo anuttaraṁ puññakkhettaṁ lokassa.
an5.139:22.1.eng: In the same way, a mendicant with five qualities is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of veneration with joined palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world.

an5.139:22.3.pli: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu khamo hoti rūpānaṁ, khamo saddānaṁ, khamo gandhānaṁ, khamo rasānaṁ, khamo phoṭṭhabbānaṁ.
an5.139:22.3.eng: It’s when a mendicant can endure sights, sounds, smells, tastes, and touches.

an5.139:23.1.pli: Kathañca, bhikkhave, bhikkhu khamo hoti rūpānaṁ?
an5.139:23.1.eng: And how is it that a mendicant can endure sights?

an5.139:23.2.pli: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu cakkhunā rūpaṁ disvā rajanīye rūpe na sārajjati, sakkoti cittaṁ samādahituṁ.
an5.139:23.2.eng: It’s when a mendicant, seeing a sight with their eyes, is not aroused by a desirable sight, so is able to still the mind.

an5.139:23.3.pli: Evaṁ kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhu khamo hoti rūpānaṁ.
an5.139:23.3.eng: That’s how a mendicant can endure sights.

an5.139:24.1.pli: Kathañca, bhikkhave, bhikkhu khamo hoti saddānaṁ?
an5.139:24.1.eng: And how is it that a mendicant can endure sounds?

an5.139:24.2.pli: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu sotena saddaṁ sutvā rajanīye sadde na sārajjati, sakkoti cittaṁ samādahituṁ.
an5.139:24.2.eng: It’s when a mendicant, hearing a sound with their ears, is not aroused by desirable sounds, so is able to still the mind.

an5.139:24.3.pli: Evaṁ kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhu khamo hoti saddānaṁ.
an5.139:24.3.eng: That’s how a mendicant can endure sounds.

an5.139:25.1.pli: Kathañca, bhikkhave, bhikkhu khamo hoti gandhānaṁ.
an5.139:25.1.eng: And how is it that a mendicant can endure smells?

an5.139:25.2.pli: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu ghānena gandhaṁ ghāyitvā rajanīye gandhe na sārajjati, sakkoti cittaṁ samādahituṁ.
an5.139:25.2.eng: It’s when a mendicant, smelling an odor with their nose, is not aroused by a desirable smell, so is able to still the mind.

an5.139:25.3.pli: Evaṁ kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhu khamo hoti gandhānaṁ.
an5.139:25.3.eng: That’s how a mendicant can endure smells.

an5.139:26.1.pli: Kathañca, bhikkhave, bhikkhu khamo hoti rasānaṁ?
an5.139:26.1.eng: And how is it that a mendicant can endure tastes?

an5.139:26.2.pli: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu jivhāya rasaṁ sāyitvā rajanīye rase na sārajjati, sakkoti cittaṁ samādahituṁ.
an5.139:26.2.eng: It’s when a mendicant, tasting a flavor with their tongue, is not aroused by desirable tastes, so is able to still the mind.

an5.139:26.3.pli: Evaṁ kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhu khamo hoti rasānaṁ.
an5.139:26.3.eng: That’s how a mendicant can endure tastes.

an5.139:27.1.pli: Kathañca, bhikkhave, bhikkhu khamo hoti phoṭṭhabbānaṁ?
an5.139:27.1.eng: And how is it that a mendicant can endure touches?

an5.139:27.2.pli: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu kāyena phoṭṭhabbaṁ phusitvā rajanīye phoṭṭhabbe na sārajjati, sakkoti cittaṁ samādahituṁ.
an5.139:27.2.eng: It’s when a mendicant, feeling a touch with their body, is not aroused by a desirable touch, so is able to still the mind.

an5.139:27.3.pli: Evaṁ kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhu khamo hoti phoṭṭhabbānaṁ.
an5.139:27.3.eng: That’s how a mendicant can endure touches.

an5.139:28.1.pli: Imehi kho, bhikkhave, pañcahi dhammehi samannāgato bhikkhu āhuneyyo hoti pāhuneyyo dakkhiṇeyyo añjalikaraṇīyo anuttaraṁ puññakkhettaṁ lokassā”ti.
an5.139:28.1.eng: A mendicant with these five qualities is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of veneration with joined palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world.”
